EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused a carport to be blown into the side of house, and |snapped numerous trees on Rocky Farm Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and moderately unstable air mass allowed for scattered afternoon thunderstorms to develop. A few of these storms became strong enough to down trees across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across Red House. Damage amounts are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two thunderstorm complexes moved around a ridge centered over the southeastern states and into Virginia. The first complex strengthened when it moved east of the Blue Ridge and produced widespread wind damage. The second complex arrived in the evening and produced flash flooding across the mountain empire of Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near Gladys. Damage amounts are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through northern Virginia, bringing showers and thunderstorms with it. A warm and humid air mass ahead of the front resulted in moderate amounts of instability. The instability combined with the cold front for a few thunderstorms to produce damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down off of Route 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms fired up initially in the afternoon of the 5th over the western mountains south of a front stalled out over the Ohio Valley. The primary severe mode was large hail over the mountains with some scattered wind damage in the piedmont.

Showers and thunderstorms developed along a passing cold front during the evening of the 28th. The lift provided by the front and a weak upper air disturbance was enough to develop the thunderstorms, and enough instability was present for some of these storms to produce damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at the intersection of Grassland Drive and Yorkshire Drive. Damage amounts are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front combined with enough afternoon heating and wind shear to generate a few hail-producing thunderstorms over parts of the New River Valley and the Rockbridge county areas on the 25th.
Scattered severe thunderstorms well in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed along Stony Point Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms well in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with a strong low pressure system pushed through the region on March 22nd. This produced severe thunderstorms in Rockbridge county that caused wind damage in the city of Lexington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds brought down numerous trees along Woods Creek Road at Washington and Lee University.  Some of the trees fell on vehicles, damaging five of them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system situated over the Ohio Valley combined with another low forming off the southeast coast to bring abundant moisture into the area. A band of thunderstorms developed during the evening of March 12th and tracked northeast into Southwest VA. A southeast flow helped enhance the rainfall over the region and |an existing snow pack in far western Giles County also helped contribute to the water runoff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused Little Creek to rise out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low over the Ohio Valley provided plenty of cold air aloft while a southerly flow ahead of this system ushered in warm and moist air from the south at the surface. The combination of the warm low-levels and the colder air aloft provided enough instability for showers and thunderstorms to develop. Some thunderstorms during the afternoon hours produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in King George.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the western Atlantic pumped in plenty of warm and humid air on the 14th. An upper-level disturbance passed through the region during the afternoon and evening hours, bringing showers and thunderstorms. Moderate to high amounts of instability was present due to relatively colder air aloft and warm, moist air at the surface. This caused thunderstorms to become severe with large hail being the primary threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of ping pong balls was reported near Reston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the western Atlantic pumped in plenty of warm and humid air on the 14th. An upper-level disturbance passed through the region during the afternoon and evening hours, bringing showers and thunderstorms. Moderate to high amounts of instability was present due to relatively colder air aloft and warm, moist air at the surface. This caused thunderstorms to become severe with large hail being the primary threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail was reported about three miles north of North Garden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms associated with low pressure and a cold front produced one to three inches of rain across portions of central and eastern Virginia from Sunday night, March 28th, through Monday afternoon March 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of one to three inches occurred across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection developed in weak to moderately unstable atmosphere south of a stationary front positioned across the southern part of West Virginia. Severe wind gusts were mainly the result of the evening convection. However...some large hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees and a few power lines were reported down in Bonny Blue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad region of thunderstorms progressed through the region. Some of these storms reached severe criteria and producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at 161 Monroe Street. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the region during the day and passed through the evening hours. Storms developed along and ahead of this front, many of which increased to severe magnitude and produce mainly large hail with some wind damage reports.
Scattered thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours across portions of Virginia. A couple of these managed to produce some hail ranging from penny to half dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail fell on Pine Hill Drive. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through the region on the 24th. Warm and humid air ahead of the cold front caused plenty of instability. The instability combined with the forcing from the front to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down in in Nellysford due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist air ahead of a cold front in the Ohio Valley combined with an upper level low pressure system to spark scattered severe thunderstorms across|southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several four inch tree limbs were blown down in yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front approached the area in the morning triggering scattered showers and thunderstorms. An outflow boundary from this precipitation area sparked additional thunderstorms in southwest Virginia, some producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on C.F. Gray Street in Brookneal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms well in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds across portions of central Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary across the area triggered isolated severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a thunderstorm wind gust to 62 mph (54 knots) 5 miles west of Gate City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The period from August 17-19 became very active hydrologically as a frontal boundary stalled out across the region and a series of upper-air impulses helped to trigger fairly widespread convection. Forecasters recognizing this potentially very wet pattern issued a Flash Flood Watch the afternoon of the 17th. By early evening the same day, thunderstorms were training over Galax, VA and vicinity with rainfall amounts up to 1.5 to 2.5 inches in several hours.  Maximum 24-hour rain gage totals ending at 12Z on the 18th within the warned area included Blackburn Knob IFLOWS 3.82���, Grayson Highland IFLOWS 3.12 and Galax Water Plant COOP 2.90���.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of streets were closed in the City of Galax due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced flash flooding across portions of southeast Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded in the City of Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Atlantic Ocean continued to pump in hot and humid air over the Mid-Atlantic. A series of upper-level disturbances in the jet stream passed through Virginia triggering showers and thunderstorms during this time. There was enough instability from the hot and humid air for some thunderstorms to contain damaging winds during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least a dozen reports of trees were down across southern Fauquier County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass remained over Virginia on the 5th. A series of upper-level disturbances in a zonal flow passed through the Mid-Atlantic during this time. Showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours. There was enough instability from the hot and humid air mass for some thunderstorms to contain damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down just north of Middleburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms associated with a cold front produced damaging winds and three tornadoes across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the Appalachian Mountains early on the morning of December 1 into an unseasonably warm and moisture atmosphere in the Mid-Atlantic. Widespread rain and thunderstorms developed, some of which produced heavy rain and flash flooding across the area. Rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to over 2 inches across most locations, with the highest report received in Rappahannock County of 3.05 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 628 at Route 630 closed due to flash flooding along Hittles Mill Stream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable atmosphere with plenty of potential for strong downdrafts of wind existed across the the region.  Strong thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and lasted into the early evening. Several of these storms realized the potential for the damaging winds and resulted in the downing of numerous trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell on Turtle Hollow Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed near the intersection of River Road and Cox Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust of 60 mph was measured at Cape Henry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The subtropical ridge was to our south while the subtropical jet remained just to our north. An upper-level disturbance in the jet stream passed through on the 22nd. Warm and humid air was in place due to a west-southwest flow around the ridge. Moderate amounts of instability along with the upper-level disturbance caused showers and thunderstorms to develop. Some thunderstorms became severe with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the western Atlantic pumped in plenty of warm and humid air on the 14th. An upper-level disturbance passed through the region during the afternoon and evening hours, bringing showers and thunderstorms. Moderate to high amounts of instability was present due to relatively colder air aloft and warm, moist air at the surface. This caused thunderstorms to become severe with large hail being the primary threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported about two miles northwest of Vienna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary across the region triggered isolated slow moving thunderstorms over the area resulting in flooding in a few locations. One person was swept away in flood waters and drowned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported heavy rain from a slow moving thunderstorm caused Manville Road northwest of Gate City to be washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms, some severe, crossed the region during the evening hours producing pockets of winds damage over a wide area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown onto power lines on Pattie Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed into the area at peak heating of the day creating high instability along with moderate wind shear. Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of the front as well as more organized lines closer to the front, producing downburst winds but very little hail. Some training of cells occurred resulting in some flash flooding as well. Every Virginia county in the warning area had a warning issued at one point or another.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were down along the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level disturbance and its associated cold front moved into Virginia on the 25th. Hot and humid conditions ahead of the front led to high amounts of instability. Showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the cold front. Due to the high amounts of instability, thunderstorms were able to mix down strong winds from aloft causing significant amounts of damage across portions of Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were down and a pine tree was snapped along Loyalty Road near Clover Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms associated with low pressure and a cold front produced one to three inches of rain across portions of central and eastern Virginia from Sunday night, March 28th, through Monday afternoon March 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of one to three inches occurred across the county. Walkerton reported 2.10 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level disturbance and its associated cold front moved into Virginia on the 25th. Hot and humid conditions ahead of the front led to high amounts of instability. Showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the cold front. Due to the high amounts of instability, thunderstorms were able to mix down strong winds from aloft causing significant amounts of damage across portions of Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were down on vehicles along Apple Pie Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the Virginia on the 28th. A southwest flow ahead of the front pumped in plenty of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico. Dewpoints were in the lower to middle 70s across most locations. The cold front combined with the warm and moist air to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Moderate to high amounts of instability along with stronger winds aloft caused some thunderstorms to produce damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown onto a house on Appling Valley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level disturbance and its associated cold front moved into Virginia on the 25th. Hot and humid conditions ahead of the front led to high amounts of instability. Showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the cold front. Due to the high amounts of instability, thunderstorms were able to mix down strong winds from aloft causing significant amounts of damage across portions of Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Branches and limbs were blown down in 60 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a deep flow of tropical moisture spreading northward along the east coast, and a near stationary frontal boundary over the region resulted in heavy rain across portions of central and eastern Virginia from Wednesday morning, September 29th, into Thursday night September 30th. Rainfall amounts ranged from four to fourteen inches over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of four to seven inches occurred across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a deep flow of tropical moisture spreading northward along the east coast, and a near stationary frontal boundary over the region resulted in heavy rain across portions of central and eastern Virginia from Wednesday morning, September 29th, into Thursday night September 30th. Rainfall amounts ranged from four to fourteen inches over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of six to twelve inches occurred across the county. Lancaster reported 10.90 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds across portions of south central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed just north of Clarksville near Highway 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed on Gaskins Road and at the intersection of Pemberton and Route 64.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure tracked across the Ohio Valley and into the Great Lakes. Southerly flow on the east side of this system brought abundant Gulf moisture into the mid-Atlantic. Heavy rain developed along a cold front, producing flash flooding and later flooding during the morning of the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A basement was flooded out and a water rescue was conducted at a residence. A rain gauge in the county measured 2.20 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm affected the eastern U.S. during the period of November 30 - December 1 as a powerful upper low and surface system moved into the Great Lakes proving an extended period of deep southerly flow across the region.  Precipitable water values were extremely high for very early December, running from 1.3 to 1.6 inches or nearly 200 percent of normal. Precipitation developed late on the 29th and produced moderate rains (0.25 to 0.50���) across the western portion of the NWS Blacksburg forecast area|ending at 7 AM on the 30th. Heavier and more widespread precipitation developed on the evening of the 30th into the early morning hours of December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along the North Fork of Catawba Creek and prompted the closure of a one lane bridge on Breckinridge Mill Road. Damage values are estimated.

Moderate to high amounts of instability was present due to relatively colder air aloft and warm, moist air at the surface. This caused thunderstorms to become severe with large hail being the primary threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters was reported about two miles northeast of Chantilly.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto Broad Run Farms. Siding was blown off of an apartment in Ashburn Village and a light pole was blown down on Sugarland Run Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance combined with weak instability to drive a squall line across portions of Southwest Virginia. Very strong winds aloft were able to mix down to the surface leading to many reports of downed trees. This line weakened as it approached the mountains, with no severe reports east of the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One large tree was blown down at the corner of Wadsworth Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms well in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of central and eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris was blown onto track of roller coaster. Five people were injured.
